pairs.definition_drop: Whether the household includes a healthcare, emergency-response, or direct community-service professional who can provide concrete on-site benefits — first-aid infrastructure, CPR training, social-service navigation, community interpretation — beyond standard maintenance.
pairs.definition_keep: Whether either adult holds a health or emergency-response professional background — nursing, paramedic, occupational health — that can be deployed for co-op member benefit through first-aid organisation, CPR instruction, or on-call health advice.
pairs.reason: Both definitions identically describe a household member with healthcare/emergency-response credentials who can deliver on-site co-op benefits (first-aid infrastructure, CPR training), and would score any given applicant the same way for the same reason.

pairs.definition_drop: The degree to which the applicant describes valuing and actively nurturing relationships with neighbours and co-residents — through hospitality, care, and genuine community-building — beyond fulfilling formal obligations.
pairs.definition_keep: The degree to which the applicant's stated motivation for seeking co-op housing centres on cooperative values — mutual aid, shared responsibility, community-building — as ends in themselves, as opposed to primarily instrumental goals such as rent affordability or housing stability.
pairs.reason: An ideologically committed cooperativist who values governance and mutual aid abstractly but is personally reserved and introverted would score high on intrinsic motivation yet low on interpersonal warmth and relational nurturing.

pairs.definition_drop: The ratio of total household income to the number of dependants, capturing how much income is available per person — a proxy for whether the household has practical financial flexibility after basic family costs and housing charges.
pairs.definition_keep: The total annual household income relative to the applicant pool, indicating the financial cushion available to meet housing charges reliably and absorb unexpected levies or temporary income disruption without default risk.
pairs.name_drop: Financial Headroom Relative to Household Size
pairs.reason: A high-income household with five dependants scores high on total income headroom but potentially low on per-person headroom once family costs are factored in, making these genuinely distinct measures of financial capacity.

pairs.definition_drop: The count of dependent children in the household, affecting demand on shared spaces, the social texture of the co-op, and the household's available adult time for co-op obligations.
pairs.definition_keep: The total number of people in the household (adults plus children), relevant to unit occupancy, space needs, and — in larger households — the potential for multiple adults to contribute to co-op work.
pairs.reason: A household of four adults and zero children scores high on total household size but zero on dependent-children count, so the two axes diverge concretely and measure different things (total occupancy vs. child-specific demand).

pairs.definition_drop: The degree to which the applicant explicitly commits — in their own words — to the full range of co-op participation obligations: work hours, committee roles, AGM attendance, and shared-governance duties, framed as a personal value rather than an obligation to be minimised.
pairs.definition_keep: The degree to which the applicant's stated motivation for seeking co-op housing centres on cooperative values — mutual aid, shared responsibility, community-building — as ends in themselves, as opposed to primarily instrumental goals such as rent affordability or housing stability.
pairs.name_drop: Stated Commitment to Active Co-op Participation
pairs.reason: An applicant deeply motivated by cooperative values could still be vague about specific participation pledges (work hours, committees), while a pragmatically motivated applicant could explicitly enumerate every obligation — scoring high on commitment but low on intrinsic motivation.

pairs.definition_drop: The degree to which the application essays are grounded in a genuine personal voice, concrete verifiable experiences, named organisations, and specific past outcomes — as opposed to generic language, corporate jargon, or terse non-answers that leave the committee unable to assess fit.
pairs.definition_keep: The degree to which the applicant's stated motivation for seeking co-op housing centres on cooperative values — mutual aid, shared responsibility, community-building — as ends in themselves, as opposed to primarily instrumental goals such as rent affordability or housing stability.
pairs.reason: A genuinely values-driven applicant could write in generic, abstract language (high motivation, low specificity), while a pragmatically motivated applicant seeking affordable housing could write a highly concrete, detailed essay (low motivation, high specificity).

pairs.definition_drop: The extent to which the household specifically seeks and values the democratic, member-governed character of co-ops — wanting a direct say in how the community is run — as distinct from the social or financial benefits of co-op living.
pairs.definition_keep: The degree to which the applicant's stated motivation for seeking co-op housing centres on cooperative values — mutual aid, shared responsibility, community-building — as ends in themselves, as opposed to primarily instrumental goals such as rent affordability or housing stability.
pairs.reason: An applicant motivated primarily by communal living and mutual aid could be indifferent to formal governance and voting rights, scoring high on intrinsic motivation but low on democratic governance valuation, and vice versa for someone who specifically seeks member-control but is less interested in community-building.

settled.decision: R2 uniquely splits licensed from unlicensed practical skills, a distinction supported by a real applicant difference: a licensed electrician can legally do panel work a skilled carpenter cannot. R2's licensed_building_trade_skills is kept as its own axis. R0:hands_on_trade_skill_depth, R1:physical_trade_skills, R3:hands_on_trade_skills, and R4:hands_on_trade_depth all conflate licensed and unlicensed — their licensed-credential content is absorbed here; their unlicensed/practical content is absorbed into practical_building_maintenance_skill below.
settled.name: Licensed / Certified Trade Skills

settled.decision: R2:practical_building_maintenance_capability is the sole source that explicitly frames unlicensed practical skills as a separate axis from licensed trades. A mason/tiler or carpenter can reduce contractor spend meaningfully without holding any licence, making this genuinely distinct from the licensed axis. The unlicensed/practical content embedded in the multi-concept axes (R0:hands_on_trade_skill_depth, R1:physical_trade_skills, R3:hands_on_trade_skills, R4:hands_on_trade_depth) is absorbed here; their licensed content went to licensed_trade_skills above.
settled.name: Practical (Unlicensed) Building Maintenance Skill

settled.decision: All five reports surface an identical outdoor/grounds/horticulture concept (R0:grounds_and_horticulture_skill, R1:grounds_and_outdoor_skills, R2:grounds_outdoor_maintenance_skill, R3:grounds_and_outdoor_maintenance_skills, R4:grounds_and_horticulture_skills). They would score the same applicant identically. Merged into one axis; R0's key is reused as it is the most concise.
settled.name: Grounds & Horticulture Skill

settled.decision: This is a committee-kept axis. R0:governance_and_financial_admin_experience, R0:analogous_collective_governance_experience, R1:coop_governance_experience_depth (its non-housing-co-op content), R2:transferable_governance_experience, R3:shared_governance_experience_depth, and R4:adjacent_governance_experience all measure transferable governance experience in non-housing-co-op settings — exactly what this kept axis defines. Financial skill is split off into financial_governance_skill below; meeting-process skill is split off into governance_process_skill below. The governance-experience-breadth content from all these sources is absorbed here. Kept axis key and definition preserved.
settled.name: Formal Collective Governance Experience

settled.decision: R0:prior_housing_coop_experience, R1:residential_coop_lived_experience, R3:coop_cultural_familiarity, and R4:housing_coop_experience_directness all measure the same thing: direct housing-co-op residence and/or governance, as distinct from analogous-but-not-housing governance (captured in governance_board_experience). A person could score high on governance_board_experience (strata treasurer for 10 years) and low here (never lived in a housing co-op), making these genuinely distinct axes. Merged into one axis under R0's key.
settled.name: Housing Co-op Lived & Governance Experience

settled.decision: R0:financial_bookkeeping_skill, R2:financial_administrative_skill, R3:financial_governance_skills, and R4:governance_financial_skills all measure the same financial-stewardship concept. R1:financial_and_admin_governance_skills and R0:governance_and_financial_admin_experience bundle financial skill with governance process; their financial content is absorbed here, their governance-process content goes to governance_process_skill. Merged under a new key that captures the settled concept precisely.
settled.name: Financial Governance & Bookkeeping Skill

settled.decision: R3:governance_process_skills isolates meeting-management and procedural skill from financial governance — a person can be an expert chair and minute-keeper while having no financial acumen, and vice versa, making these genuinely distinct. The committee-kept axis governance_board_experience captures *experience* in governance bodies; this axis captures *process-management skill* specifically. The governance-process content embedded in R0:governance_and_financial_admin_experience and R1:financial_and_admin_governance_skills is absorbed here.
settled.name: Governance Process & Meeting Management Skill

settled.decision: R0:conflict_mediation_skill, R1:conflict_mediation_and_hr_skills, R3:interpersonal_mediation_skills are all identical. R4:conflict_mediation_and_social_skills bundles mediation with onboarding and volunteer-engagement skills; its mediation content is absorbed here, its onboarding content goes to member_onboarding_experience, and its volunteer-engagement content goes to volunteer_engagement_burnout_awareness. R2:health_safety_community_service_skill bundles nursing, social work, interpretation, and mediation; its mediation content is absorbed here, its health/safety content goes to health_and_safety_skill.
settled.name: Conflict Mediation & HR Skill

settled.decision: R0:health_and_safety_skill and R1:community_health_and_safety_skills are identical. R2:health_safety_community_service_skill additionally includes social work and interpretation alongside medical skills — these are all professional qualifications that translate into direct community-safety or social-service benefits for residents and are plausibly scored together (a social worker and a nurse both score high; neither a carpenter nor a software developer does). The mediation sub-content of R2 was split off into conflict_mediation_skill above.
settled.name: Health, Safety & Community-Service Professional Skill

settled.decision: R0:digital_and_tech_infrastructure_skill, R1:tech_and_digital_infrastructure_skills, R2:digital_infrastructure_skill, R3:digital_and_admin_infrastructure_skills, and R4:digital_and_systems_skills are all identical. Merged under R0's key. R3's label includes 'administrative infrastructure' (spreadsheets, records) but this is within scope of the settled definition.
settled.name: Digital & Tech Infrastructure Skill

settled.decision: R0:repair_coordination_and_contractor_management, R1:maintenance_coordination_skills, R3:maintenance_coordination_skills, and R4:maintenance_scheduling_and_procurement_skills are all identical — they measure the administrative coordination side of building maintenance, distinct from doing the physical work. R4:maintenance_scheduling_and_procurement_skills is a near-verbatim re-carving. Merged under R0's key.
settled.name: Repair Coordination & Contractor Management Skill

settled.decision: R0:community_programming_contribution, R1:community_social_programming_capacity, R2:volunteer_program_delivery_capacity, R3:programmatic_community_contribution, and R4:family_and_children_program_capacity all measure the same capacity to *run co-op-facing programs*, as distinct from external community engagement (which goes to volunteer_civic_engagement). R4's axis focuses on family/children programs, which is a subset; it scores the same applicants high as the broader axis. Merged under R0's key.
settled.name: Community Program Delivery Capacity

settled.decision: R0:member_onboarding_and_orientation_experience, R1:new_member_integration_orientation, R2:member_onboarding_orientation_capacity are all identical. This is genuinely distinct from general community programming (it specifically concerns integrating *new members* into the co-op's operational systems) and from conflict mediation or volunteer-engagement management. Kept as its own axis under R0's key.
settled.name: New-Member Onboarding & Integration Experience

settled.decision: This is a committee-kept axis. R0:social_integration_and_outreach_orientation, R1:community_enrichment_activities (the external-engagement sub-content), R2:community_engagement_breadth, R3:community_engagement_outside_coop, and R4:community_embeddedness_outside_home all measure the same external prosocial engagement concept. Distinct from community_programming_contribution (which is about running programs *at the co-op*): a person could be deeply embedded in external volunteer life but offer no specific co-op programming, and vice versa. Kept axis key, name, and definition preserved; all source keys absorbed.
settled.name: Broader Volunteer & Civic Engagement

settled.decision: This is a committee-kept axis. R0:cooperative_ideology_alignment, R1:cooperative_values_alignment, R2:cooperative_values_articulation, R3:cooperative_motivation_alignment (report 3), R4:cooperative_motivation_alignment, and R4:cooperative_reciprocity_framing all measure whether the applicant's motivation is intrinsically cooperative or purely instrumental. R4:cooperative_reciprocity_framing (relational vs. transactional framing) is a sub-dimension of this same concept — an applicant who scores high on intrinsic values alignment necessarily frames participation reciprocally; the two axes would score the same applicants essentially identically. Merged into kept axis; all source keys absorbed. Kept axis key, name, and definition preserved.
settled.name: Intrinsic Co-op Philosophy Alignment

settled.decision: This is a committee-kept axis. R0:conflict_history_and_collegiality, R1:collaboration_track_record, R2:conflict_resolution_orientation, R3:conflict_collaborative_posture, and R4:governance_conflict_record all measure the same track record of constructive collaborative behaviour in governance settings. Distinct from conflict_mediation_skill (professional skill to mediate *others'* disputes): an applicant can have poor personal conflict history and still be a professionally trained mediator, or be consistently collegial without any mediation training. Kept axis key, name, and definition preserved; all source keys absorbed.
settled.name: Collaborative Disposition in Shared Governance

settled.decision: This is a committee-kept axis. R0:stated_willingness_to_participate_in_governance, R1:participation_commitment, R2:stated_participation_commitment, and R3:work_hour_commitment_intent all measure the applicant's stated *willingness* to participate, independent of whether they have the time. Distinct from available_time_for_coop_work (which measures whether they actually *can*, given their schedule): a person can be highly willing but genuinely time-constrained, or have abundant time but state minimal commitment intent. Kept axis key, name, and definition preserved; all source keys absorbed.
settled.name: Stated Commitment to Active Co-op Participation

settled.decision: This is a committee-kept axis. R0:self_reported_availability_for_coop_work, R1:discretionary_time_availability, R2:available_discretionary_time, R3:work_hour_availability, and R4:stated_availability_for_coop_work are all identical — they measure *actual available time*, distinct from stated willingness (stated_participation_commitment). Kept axis key, name, and definition preserved; all source keys absorbed.
settled.name: Realistic Available Time for Co-op Work

settled.decision: R4:on_call_emergency_willingness is unique to R4 and genuinely distinct from available_time_for_coop_work (which covers scheduled work hours and committee service) and stated_participation_commitment (which covers planned obligations). An applicant could have abundant planned availability but strongly prefer not to be on-call, or vice versa. Kept as its own axis.
settled.name: On-Call Emergency Availability

settled.decision: R0:long_term_tenure_intent, R1:long_term_residency_intent, R2:long_term_stay_intention, R3:long_term_residency_intent, and R4:long_term_residential_intent are all identical. Merged under R0's key.
settled.name: Long-Term Tenure Intent

settled.decision: R0:knowledge_transfer_and_documentation_habit, R1:knowledge_transfer_orientation, R2:knowledge_transfer_orientation, R3:knowledge_transfer_orientation, and R4:knowledge_transfer_and_documentation_ethic are all identical. Merged under R0's key.
settled.name: Knowledge Transfer & Documentation Practice

settled.decision: R0:volunteer_engagement_and_burnout_awareness is unique to R0 and captures an organisational-health concept not covered by any other axis. It is genuinely distinct from stated_participation_commitment (personal willingness to show up), available_time_for_coop_work (personal capacity), and community_programming_contribution (running programs). A person can be a dedicated personal contributor who never thinks about the broader volunteer ecology. Kept as its own axis. The volunteer/social-skill content in R4:conflict_mediation_and_social_skills that touched on keeping volunteers engaged is absorbed here.
settled.name: Volunteer Engagement & Burnout Prevention Awareness

settled.decision: R0:application_transparency_and_candor and R1:candor_about_limitations are identical — both measure proactive disclosure of weaknesses and constraints. Distinct from essay_claim_concreteness (specificity of positive claims): an applicant can be very candid about limitations while being vague about contributions, or vice versa. Merged under R0's key.
settled.name: Application Transparency & Candour

settled.decision: R2:specificity_of_contribution_offer, R4:essay_claim_concreteness, and R1:application_engagement_depth all measure the concreteness and specificity of how applicants describe their contributions — distinct from candour about limitations (application_transparency_and_candor). Merged under R4's key as the most precise label.
settled.name: Specificity & Concreteness of Contribution Claims

settled.decision: R0:environmental_stewardship_orientation and R2:environmental_stewardship_ethic are identical. Merged under R0's key.
settled.name: Environmental Stewardship Orientation

settled.decision: R0:income_level_housing_charge_headroom, R1:income_to_housing_charge_headroom, R2:income_to_housing_charge_headroom (report 2), R3:income_financial_buffer, and R4:household_income_headroom all measure absolute income relative to housing charges. Merged under R0's key.
settled.name: Income Level: Housing Charge Headroom

settled.decision: R0:income_stability_dual_earner_resilience, R1:income_concentration_risk, R2:income_stability_dual_earner, R3:income_concentration_risk, and R4:dual_earner_balance all measure the same income-distribution concept. Distinct from income_level_housing_charge_headroom (absolute income level): a household can have a high total income that is entirely concentrated in one earner. Merged under R0's key.
settled.name: Dual-Earner Income Resilience

settled.decision: R0:employment_tenure_stability, R1:employment_tenure_stability, R2:employment_tenure_stability, R3:employment_tenure_stability, and R4:household_employment_tenure are all identical. Merged under the shared key.
settled.name: Employment Tenure Stability

settled.decision: R2:household_size_income_ratio and R4:income_per_household_member are identical per-capita income concepts, genuinely distinct from income_level_housing_charge_headroom (which is absolute income) because a large household on a high absolute income can still face high per-capita stress. Merged under a new key. R1:household_occupancy_size captures infrastructure demand (shared laundry, storage load), not financial pressure — kept as a separate axis below.
settled.name: Per-Capita Income Adequacy

settled.decision: R1:household_occupancy_size is unique to R1 and captures infrastructure-demand intensity, distinct from per_capita_income_adequacy (which is about financial pressure) — a small high-income household and a small low-income household both place the same infrastructure load despite very different financial profiles. Kept as its own axis.
settled.name: Household Occupancy Size

settled.decision: This is a committee-kept axis. R0:existing_connection_to_this_coop, R1:insider_connection_to_coop, R2:insider_familiarity_with_this_coop, R3:existing_coop_social_connection, and R4:existing_penta_connection are all identical. Kept axis key, name, and definition preserved; all source keys absorbed.
settled.name: Existing Personal Connection to This Co-op

settled.decision: R3 uniquely splits child age into two axes — near-term participation readiness vs. long-term household stability horizon — and provides evidence they diverge: a household with toddlers scores LOW on participation readiness now but HIGH on stability horizon (many years of family life ahead), while a household with teenagers scores differently on both. R0:child_age_profile_community_contribution, R1:child_age_profile, and R2:child_age_profile_community_impact all treat this as a single axis closer to participation readiness. Their content is absorbed here; the stability-horizon content goes to child_household_stability_horizon.
settled.name: Children's Age — Near-Term Community Participation Readiness

settled.decision: R3:child_household_stability_horizon captures the *stability horizon* dimension of children's ages, which is genuinely distinct from near-term participation readiness — a household with toddlers scores LOW on participation readiness but HIGH on stability horizon, while a household with teenagers is the reverse. Kept as a separate axis per R3's analysis.
settled.name: Children's Age — Household Stability Horizon

settled.decision: R2:family_oriented_community_motivation is unique to R2. It is genuinely distinct from coop_motivation_intrinsic (a household with deep co-op philosophy may have no children and never mention this motivation) and from long_term_tenure_intent (a household can signal long-term intent without framing it through children). An applicant could score high here and low on coop_motivation_intrinsic (family-centred but not philosophically cooperative), or vice versa. Kept as its own axis.
settled.name: Family / Child-Centred Co-op Motivation

settled.decision: R2:pet_situation_clarity is unique to R2 and captures a practical compliance-assessment axis not covered by any other dimension. It does not overlap with any other settled axis. Kept as its own axis.
settled.name: Pet Situation Clarity

dimensions.definition: Whether either adult holds a formal trade licence or professional certification (electrical, plumbing, HVAC, gas, Red Seal) directly applicable to building systems, enabling the co-op to respond to maintenance needs without paid contractors.
dimensions.high_end: At least one adult holds one or more trade licences or Red Seal certifications covering major building systems (electrical, plumbing, mechanical), with explicit readiness to apply them on-site.
dimensions.low_end: Neither adult holds any trade licence or certification; household explicitly acknowledges inability to perform licensed building repairs.
dimensions.name: Licensed / Certified Trade Skills

dimensions.definition: Hands-on ability to perform routine, non-licensed building and grounds-adjacent maintenance — carpentry, painting, tiling, masonry, small-engine servicing, tool repair — reducing reliance on paid contractors for everyday upkeep.
dimensions.high_end: Articulates specific, credible non-licensed maintenance skills (tiling and masonry, carpentry and painting, tool and small-engine servicing) with a demonstrated track record of applying them in shared or multi-unit settings.
dimensions.low_end: No practical maintenance skills offered; explicitly defers all repair work to paid contractors or offers only vague 'will do my share' language with no specifics.
dimensions.name: Practical (Unlicensed) Building Maintenance Skill

dimensions.definition: Whether the household can contribute skilled labour to outdoor and grounds upkeep — arboriculture, horticulture, composting, food gardening, storm-damage clearance, landscaping — that would otherwise require hired specialists.
dimensions.high_end: At least one adult is a certified arborist and/or experienced year-round gardener who explicitly offers to manage shared beds, compost, tree health, and grounds safety after weather events.
dimensions.low_end: No adult mentions any outdoor or grounds skills; contributions are limited to generic unskilled tasks or nothing outdoors is mentioned at all.
dimensions.name: Outdoor & Grounds Maintenance Skills

dimensions.definition: Experience serving in formal leadership or committee roles in a shared-governance body — co-op board, strata council, tenants' association executive, childcare co-op board — including managing agendas, running elections, keeping minutes, and navigating collective decision-making. Does not require the body to be a residential housing co-op.
dimensions.high_end: Served multiple terms on a formal board or council with demonstrated institutional knowledge: chairing meetings, conflict resolution, volunteer engagement, hand-off practices, and sustained stewardship of a collective body.
dimensions.low_end: No formal governance experience of any kind in any collective or community organisation; has not sat on any board, committee, or council.
dimensions.name: Formal Collective Governance Experience

dimensions.definition: The depth and directness of prior experience living in or holding a governance role within a housing co-op specifically — including first-hand knowledge of work hours, shared decision-making, consensus cycles, and co-op rhythms.
dimensions.high_end: Grew up in or lived in a housing co-op for multiple years, and/or served multiple terms in a formal co-op board or committee role, with demonstrated outcomes such as passed audits or documented processes handed to successors.
dimensions.low_end: No prior experience living in or governing any housing co-op; all co-op knowledge is second-hand, theoretical, or limited to analogous settings.
dimensions.name: Housing Co-op Governance & Living Experience

dimensions.definition: Depth of hands-on experience managing or auditing collective finances — bookkeeping, budget preparation, account reconciliation, audit readiness, and treasurer roles in a co-op or comparable shared-governance organisation.
dimensions.high_end: Has served as treasurer of a multi-unit co-op or organisation through multiple clean audits, prepared financial statements, reconciled accounts, and mentored a successor on financial records — or works professionally as a bookkeeper and has offered this skill to the co-op.
dimensions.low_end: No financial, bookkeeping, or collective budget experience of any kind; no offer of audit, accounts, or budget support made.
dimensions.name: Financial Governance & Bookkeeping Experience

dimensions.definition: Capacity to run or meaningfully contribute to formal collective governance processes — chairing meetings, writing minutes, conducting elections, managing agendas, and keeping group decision-making orderly and transparent.
dimensions.high_end: Has chaired meetings, kept clean minutes, run elections fairly, and managed long agenda-driven processes across multiple governance bodies; can hold a room and keep process transparent without supervision.
dimensions.low_end: No experience in any formal governance role; no described capacity for running or supporting structured collective decision-making.
dimensions.name: Governance Process & Meeting Management Skill

dimensions.definition: Whether the household holds formal professional or demonstrably practised experience mediating interpersonal disputes, facilitating difficult conversations, managing volunteer burnout, or applying HR-level conflict resolution in a shared-living or community context.
dimensions.high_end: Holds a professional background in HR mediation or dispute resolution, or has directly and demonstrably mediated member or neighbour disputes as a board or committee member, and has offered this capacity explicitly to the co-op.
dimensions.low_end: No described experience in professional mediation, conflict resolution, or HR-level people management; any conflict experience is as a party to a dispute rather than as a facilitator.
dimensions.name: Professional Conflict Mediation & HR Skills

dimensions.definition: Whether the household can build or maintain digital tools that reduce administrative friction for the co-op — booking systems, maintenance-request platforms, newsletters, signage, financial spreadsheets, or communications design.
dimensions.high_end: Software developer or UX/graphic designer who explicitly offers to build and maintain a bespoke co-op management system, or provides design and spreadsheet infrastructure that the co-op would otherwise outsource.
dimensions.low_end: No digital, design, or systems-building skills described; no offer of any contribution to co-op digital or administrative infrastructure.
dimensions.name: Digital & Tech Infrastructure Skills

dimensions.definition: Experience managing the administrative side of building upkeep — scheduling tradespeople, soliciting and comparing quotes, tracking warranties, following repairs to completion, and documenting outcomes for future volunteers. Distinct from performing the physical work oneself.
dimensions.high_end: Has directly chaired or managed a maintenance committee or equivalent role, scheduling trades, obtaining multiple quotes, tracking repair warranties and work orders to completion over an extended period.
dimensions.low_end: No experience coordinating or procuring building maintenance work; any contribution is limited to direct personal labour or is entirely absent.
dimensions.name: Maintenance Coordination & Procurement Experience

dimensions.definition: Whether the household can design and deliver programs for co-op members — children's activities, educational workshops, shared-meal coordination, sports coaching, language and cultural events — that build community life beyond maintenance obligations.
dimensions.high_end: Has professional skills directly applicable to resident programming (teacher offering homework clubs, cook offering shared-meal coordination, sports coach offering youth activities) and has specifically proposed running such programs as a co-op contribution.
dimensions.low_end: No educational, recreational, cooking, or community-programming skill or offer mentioned; contributions framed purely as physical maintenance or administrative tasks.
dimensions.name: Community Programming & Social Infrastructure Capacity

dimensions.definition: Experience designing or directly running new-member orientation programs, managing work-shift schedules, and helping newcomers integrate into a co-operative community — as distinct from managing ongoing volunteer retention.
dimensions.high_end: Has directly run new-member orientation programs, managed work-shift rosters for a co-op or association, and explicitly volunteers to take on this role, making new members feel welcomed and engaged from day one.
dimensions.low_end: No experience with member onboarding, orientation, or work-shift coordination; no offer to take on this function.
dimensions.name: Member Onboarding & Orientation Skill

dimensions.definition: The range and regularity of the household's voluntary contributions to community life outside the co-op — coaching, charity events, repair cafés, arts groups, free classes, community interpretation — as evidence of a sustained community-oriented disposition.
dimensions.high_end: Actively and regularly engaged in multiple forms of outside community volunteering (e.g. youth sports coaching, charity race marshalling, repair café participation, community orchestra fundraising, teaching free classes) demonstrating an ingrained habit of civic contribution.
dimensions.low_end: Little or no evidence of voluntary community involvement outside the home; household presents only private hobbies or family life with no outward-facing volunteering.
dimensions.name: Broader Volunteer & Civic Engagement

dimensions.definition: The degree to which the applicant's stated motivation for seeking co-op housing centres on cooperative values — mutual aid, shared responsibility, community-building — as ends in themselves, as opposed to primarily instrumental goals such as rent affordability or housing stability.
dimensions.high_end: Articulates a genuine co-operative philosophy lived independently of this application — mutual aid over individualism, democratic responsibility as meaningful in itself — demonstrated through past behaviour such as food sharing, tool libraries, or carpool networks.
dimensions.low_end: States that cost or stability is the primary or exclusive driver; co-op participation is framed as a necessary condition of the housing deal rather than a desired feature in itself ('mainly the rent is cheap'; 'we'd save the co-op money — that's it really').
dimensions.name: Intrinsic Co-op Philosophy Alignment

dimensions.definition: The degree to which an applicant demonstrates comfort and patience working within collective decision-making processes — accepting majority outcomes, engaging respectfully with disagreement, and supporting the group's process even when it moves slowly.
dimensions.high_end: Explicitly describes learning to disagree in a meeting and leave as friends, valuing the patience that consensus requires, or framing the democratic process of co-op governance as a feature rather than a friction.
dimensions.low_end: Describes impatience with group processes, a tendency to take over when others seem disorganised, or explicit refusal to participate in meetings and collective decisions.
dimensions.name: Collaborative Disposition in Shared Governance

dimensions.definition: The degree to which the applicant explicitly commits — in their own words — to the full range of co-op participation obligations: work hours, committee roles, AGM attendance, and shared-governance duties, framed as a personal value rather than an obligation to be minimised.
dimensions.high_end: Explicitly volunteers for maintenance rotations, committee seats, AGMs, and work days; uses language like 'never miss a work day' or 'take on more than our share'; commitment is unconditional and proactively stated.
dimensions.low_end: Explicitly declines committee involvement or AGM attendance, states intention to do only the minimum, or signals plans to exit once a personal financial goal is met.
dimensions.name: Stated Commitment to Active Co-op Participation

dimensions.definition: How much discretionary time the household realistically has for co-op shifts and committee work after accounting for self-reported external commitments — employment demands, outside volunteer roles, touring schedules, board seats elsewhere, and caregiving obligations.
dimensions.high_end: Explicitly reports sparse outside commitments and abundant free time; no competing obligations disclosed; states willingness to take on more than the minimum share of co-op work hours.
dimensions.low_end: Self-reports a genuinely packed schedule (multiple outside boards, choir touring, repair café plus other boards) that would leave only minimum hours available for co-op duties.
dimensions.name: Realistic Available Time for Co-op Work

dimensions.definition: Whether the household explicitly offers to be available on short notice for small building emergencies outside of scheduled work hours.
dimensions.high_end: Both adults explicitly state they are comfortable being on the on-call list for small emergencies and can respond without supervision.
dimensions.low_end: No mention of emergency or on-call availability; the household's contribution is limited to scheduled shifts only.
dimensions.name: On-Call / Emergency Availability

dimensions.definition: Whether the household signals an intention to remain in the co-op as a stable, long-term home rather than treating membership as a transitional stepping stone toward another housing goal.
dimensions.high_end: Frames the co-op explicitly as a long-term or permanent home; uses language about ageing in place, raising children here over many years, or investing in the community for the long run.
dimensions.low_end: Explicitly states plans to leave once saving enough for a down payment, or otherwise frames co-op membership as a temporary cost-saving arrangement.
dimensions.name: Long-Term Residency Intent

dimensions.definition: The degree to which the applicant frames their contribution in terms of documenting processes, mentoring successors, building institutional memory, and onboarding new members — ensuring the co-op's operational knowledge survives member turnover.
dimensions.high_end: Has explicitly mentored a successor (e.g. next treasurer), written processes so the next volunteer is not lost, kept systems documented as a stated priority, or explicitly offers to run new-member orientation and onboarding.
dimensions.low_end: Contribution framed entirely in terms of personal task completion; no mention of knowledge-sharing, documentation, continuity planning, or support for incoming members.
dimensions.name: Knowledge Transfer, Documentation & Onboarding Orientation

dimensions.definition: Whether the applicant has specific experience keeping co-op or community volunteers motivated, preventing burnout, and sustaining collective participation over time — a people-management skill distinct from formal governance facilitation or professional dispute mediation.
dimensions.high_end: Explicitly describes having learned how to keep volunteers engaged without burning them out in a collective setting (e.g. childcare co-op board) and offers to apply this skill to sustain co-op participation.
dimensions.low_end: No described experience managing or retaining volunteers; applicant has not referenced the challenge of volunteer sustainability or offered any people-management capacity in this specific dimension.
dimensions.name: Volunteer Retention & Engagement Experience

dimensions.definition: Whether the applicant proactively discloses genuine limitations, scheduling conflicts, skill gaps, or past difficulties rather than presenting only a curated positive picture — enabling the committee to make an informed assessment.
dimensions.high_end: Openly names a real constraint or limitation without being asked (thin availability due to touring, minimum hours due to outside boards, a prior conflict history, explicit skill gaps), demonstrating honesty and realistic self-knowledge.
dimensions.low_end: Presents only an idealised self-portrait with no disclosed limitations, constraints, or past difficulties — making it impossible to distinguish genuine enthusiasm from overselling.
dimensions.name: Candour About Constraints & Limitations

dimensions.definition: The degree to which the application essays are grounded in a genuine personal voice, concrete verifiable experiences, named organisations, and specific past outcomes — as opposed to generic language, corporate jargon, or terse non-answers that leave the committee unable to assess fit.
dimensions.high_end: Essays are written in a clear, honest personal voice with specific details, named past roles, concrete outcomes, and evidence that allows the committee to verify claims and form a clear picture of the household's values and likely behaviour.
dimensions.low_end: Essays consist of impersonal corporate jargon, generic buzzwords, a single dismissive sentence, or extreme self-deprecation — providing no concrete basis for assessment and leaving the committee unable to evaluate the household.
dimensions.name: Essay Specificity, Authenticity & Concrete Evidence

dimensions.definition: The extent to which the household demonstrates practised, concrete environmentally conscious habits — reducing waste, composting, food growing, repair rather than replace — that would align with sustainable shared-space management.
dimensions.high_end: Demonstrates active, habitual environmental stewardship across multiple areas: packing out more than they bring, growing food on shared allotments, composting, running a repair cafe to divert waste — framed as a core household value.
dimensions.low_end: Makes no mention of environmental practices or orientation; household description focuses on other values with no environmental dimension.
dimensions.name: Environmental Stewardship Orientation

dimensions.definition: The total annual household income relative to the applicant pool, indicating the financial cushion available to meet housing charges reliably and absorb unexpected levies or temporary income disruption without default risk.
dimensions.high_end: Household income well above the pool median, providing a substantial buffer against housing charge increases, special levies, or periods of reduced income.
dimensions.low_end: Household income at the lower end of the pool, leaving limited margin to absorb unexpected costs or housing charge adjustments.
dimensions.name: Household Income Financial Headroom

dimensions.definition: How evenly household income is distributed between the two adults, measuring the household's resilience if one earner's income is interrupted through job loss, illness, parental leave, or relationship change.
dimensions.high_end: Income is split near-equally between both adults (each contributing roughly 40–60% of total), so the household can continue meeting obligations even if either income is disrupted.
dimensions.low_end: Income is heavily concentrated in one earner (one adult contributing 80%+ of total household income), making the household highly vulnerable to that earner's income being interrupted.
dimensions.name: Household Income Balance (Single-Earner Resilience)

dimensions.definition: The length of continuous employment for both adults in their current roles, jointly considered, as a proxy for income reliability and likelihood of sustained housing-charge payment capacity.
dimensions.high_end: Both adults have been continuously employed in their current roles for several years (five or more), suggesting stable, tested income streams with low near-term disruption risk.
dimensions.low_end: One or both adults started their current job very recently (within the past year or two), representing limited track record and higher uncertainty about income continuity.
dimensions.name: Employment Tenure Stability

dimensions.definition: The ratio of total household income to the number of dependants, capturing how much income is available per person — a proxy for whether the household has practical financial flexibility after basic family costs and housing charges.
dimensions.high_end: Household income is high relative to the number of adults and children, leaving meaningful financial headroom per person after family living costs and housing obligations.
dimensions.low_end: Household income is low relative to a large number of children and adults, leaving thin financial headroom per person and increasing fragility under unexpected financial demands.
dimensions.name: Financial Headroom Relative to Household Size

dimensions.definition: The total number of people in the household (adults plus children), relevant to unit occupancy, space needs, and — in larger households — the potential for multiple adults to contribute to co-op work.
dimensions.high_end: Smaller household with fewer occupants, implying lower space demand per unit and simpler occupancy logistics.
dimensions.low_end: Larger household with four or more children, implying greater space requirements and higher occupancy density.
dimensions.name: Household Size

dimensions.definition: Whether the household already has direct personal ties to current members of this specific co-op, giving them firsthand familiarity with its culture, expectations, and day-to-day operations before applying.
dimensions.high_end: Has close friends or family who are current members, has visited or spent substantial time at the co-op through those relationships, and is already familiar with its specific culture, rhythms, and community norms firsthand.
dimensions.low_end: No known existing connection to any current member; the co-op is entirely unfamiliar at a personal level; knowledge comes from the application process alone.
dimensions.name: Existing Personal Connection to This Co-op

dimensions.definition: Whether the household's children are currently at an age where they can actively participate in shared spaces, community programs, and co-op social life, and where their relative independence frees adults for co-op work contributions.
dimensions.high_end: Children are school-age (roughly 5–12), able to engage in homework clubs, youth activities, and community events, and independent enough that adults are not fully consumed by supervision.
dimensions.low_end: All children are infants or toddlers (under 3), requiring intensive supervision that may constrain adult availability; or all children are older teens (15–16), near-adult and likely to disengage from community programming imminently.
dimensions.name: Children's Age — Near-Term Community Participation Readiness

dimensions.definition: Whether the ages of the household's children indicate that the household is likely to remain at its current size and composition for many years, providing stable long-term occupancy and engagement, versus facing near-term household shrinkage as children leave home.
dimensions.high_end: Children are young (toddlers to early school age), meaning the household will remain at its current size for a decade or more — providing stable, long-term occupancy and sustained family engagement with the co-op.
dimensions.low_end: All or most children are teenagers, meaning the household will shrink substantially within a few years as children leave home, creating uncertainty about long-term fit for the unit size.
dimensions.name: Children's Age — Household Stability Horizon

dimensions.definition: Whether the applicant explicitly ties their motivation for co-op living to raising children in a known-neighbour, shared-responsibility environment — signalling investment in the community as a developmental context for the next generation.
dimensions.high_end: Explicitly frames co-op living as a desired environment for raising children — neighbours who look out for one another, modelling cooperative problem-solving — and connects this child-centred reasoning to long-term community commitment.
dimensions.low_end: No mention of children's community environment as a motivation; application is framed entirely around adult needs (affordability, stability, skills to offer) with children absent from the rationale.
dimensions.name: Family / Child-Centred Co-op Motivation

dimensions.definition: How clearly defined the household's pet situation is relative to co-op pet policies — whether the animal situation is known and stated, or uncertain and unresolved.
dimensions.high_end: Pet situation is clearly defined as none, or as a specific named animal the household already has — no ambiguity about what animals will be in the unit.
dimensions.low_end: Pet situation is explicitly undecided ('not sure yet') or ambiguously described, leaving open the question of what animals will be present and whether they align with co-op rules.
dimensions.name: Pet Situation Certainty

dimensions.definition: Whether either adult holds a health or emergency-response professional background — nursing, paramedic, occupational health — that can be deployed for co-op member benefit through first-aid organisation, CPR instruction, or on-call health advice.
dimensions.high_end: Registered nurse or equivalent with explicit plans to establish a first-aid station, run CPR refreshers for members, or otherwise extend professional health skills to co-op benefit.
dimensions.low_end: No health or emergency-response professional background relevant to community benefit; no offer of health or safety infrastructure.
dimensions.name: Community Health & Safety Capacity
